text: Cognitive HCI theories go beyond this, if you're willing to admit things that aren't behavioral "laws" with well-defined numbers. For example, <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_foraging|Information Foraging> describes how people engage with graph-like information environments and is pretty well-established and has been found to apply in many contexts (reading code, browsing the web, skimming search engine results, searching wikis, etc.). This gives an answer to questions like "Why is it helpful that Google's search results page includes excerpts from the pages?" The answer is that it helps provide a stronger "information scent".

text: In the data visualization world, there's the <https://www.cs.umd.edu/~ben/papers/Shneiderman1996eyes.pdf|Visual Information-Seeking Mantra> ("Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand"), which is a good guide for many different applications across domains (data dashboards, google maps, really any interactive data viz)
